ideas.Man is the measure of all things:
"**Man is the measure of all things**" (*pantōn chrēmatōn metron anthrōpos*) is the most famous saying of Protagoras and one of the most celebrated and debated pronouncements in all of Greek philosophy — the opening line of his lost work *Truth*, and the classic statement of philosophical **relativism**. The fuller version runs: "Man is the measure of all things — of the things that are, that they are, and of the things that are not, that they are not."

The dictum asserts that **human beings** are the standard or criterion by which reality and truth are judged: things are however they *appear to* the perceiving, judging person. There is no absolute, objective truth existing independently of human perception and belief; rather, what is true is true *for* the individual (or, on some readings, for the community) to whom it appears so. Protagoras's own illustration concerns perception: the same wind may feel cold to one person and warm to another, and — crucially — it *is* cold for the one and warm for the other; neither is mistaken, because there is no "wind-in-itself" that is really one temperature independent of how it is experienced.

Extended beyond perception to values and beliefs, the doctrine implies that truth, goodness, justice, and beauty are all **relative** to the perceiver or the culture, with no view being simply, objectively correct. This posed a profound challenge that **Plato** took with great seriousness, devoting much of his dialogue *Theaetetus* to refuting it (arguing, among other things, that it is self-refuting: if all views are equally true, then so is the view that Protagoras's relativism is false). Whether Protagoras meant an individual or a collective "measure," and how radical his relativism was, remain debated — but the saying endures as the founding formula of relativism.
ideas.Relativism about truth and value:
Protagoras, the most famous of the **Sophists**, is celebrated (and was notorious) as the great early exponent of **relativism** — the view that **truth and value are not absolute** but relative to individuals, cultures, or perspectives, so that there is no single objective standard by which all beliefs and practices can be measured. Grounded in his dictum that "man is the measure of all things," Protagorean relativism applied across knowledge, morality, and religion, and it made him a pivotal and controversial figure in the intellectual ferment of fifth-century Athens.

In the realm of **truth and perception**, Protagoras held that things are as they appear to each perceiver, so that contradictory judgments can both be "true" — true *for* the respective individuals — with no fact of the matter beyond appearances. In the realm of **values, law, and custom**, his relativism took a more cultural form: different societies have different laws, customs, and conceptions of justice and virtue, and none is simply, universally correct; what is "just" or "right" is relative to the norms of a given community (*nomos*). This challenged the traditional assumption that moral and political norms are grounded in a fixed natural or divine order.

Protagoras's relativism was not merely destructive, however. He seems to have held that, while no belief is truer than another, some are **better** — more useful, more beneficial — than others, and that the wise person (and the good sophist or teacher) can help individuals and cities replace worse beliefs and conditions with better ones, much as a doctor restores health. His relativism thus left room for practical wisdom and social improvement. Enormously influential and much attacked (especially by Plato), Protagorean relativism raised enduring questions about objectivity, culture, and the foundations of knowledge and morality.
ideas.Rhetoric and argument from both sides:
Protagoras was a master and teacher of **rhetoric** and **argumentation**, and among his most characteristic and influential doctrines was the principle that "there are **two arguments** (*dissoi logoi*) concerning every matter, opposed to each other" — that on any question, opposing cases can be constructed, and that a skilled speaker can argue persuasively on *both* sides. This teaching lay at the heart of the Sophists' educational program and their profession of preparing young men for success in the law courts and political assembly of democratic Athens.

The doctrine follows naturally from Protagoras's relativism: if there is no single objective truth, and things are as they appear to different people, then genuinely opposed accounts of any matter can each have their own validity, and the task is not to discover the one truth but to argue effectively. Protagoras therefore trained his students to develop and deploy arguments on either side of a question, cultivating the flexibility, verbal skill, and persuasive power (*peithō*) essential to public life in a participatory democracy where success depended on winning debates and swaying juries and assemblies.

This teaching earned the Sophists their lasting (and often unfair) reputation for intellectual dishonesty. Protagoras was accused — notably of promising to "make the **weaker argument the stronger**," a phrase that came to symbolize the supposedly amoral, manipulative rhetoric of the Sophists, teaching people to win arguments regardless of truth or justice (Aristophanes lampooned it, and it was among the charges later leveled against Socrates by association). Whether Protagoras meant something more innocent — teaching the fuller examination of both sides of a case, as any good education in reasoning might — is debated. Either way, his emphasis on the two-sidedness of every question and the power of argument profoundly shaped the development of rhetoric, dialectic, and debate.

works.Truth (fragments):
*Truth* (*Alētheia*), also known as the "Throws" (*Kataballontes*, "Overthrowing Arguments"), was the major philosophical work of Protagoras — and, though it survives only in **fragments** and reports, it is one of the most important texts of the Sophistic movement, for it opened with the single most famous sentence in Sophistic philosophy. Like nearly all Sophistic writings, the complete work is lost (Protagoras's books were reportedly even publicly burned in Athens on account of his religious skepticism), and we know it chiefly through the discussions of later authors, above all Plato.

The treatise began with Protagoras's celebrated declaration of **relativism**: "**Man is the measure of all things** — of the things that are, that they are, and of the things that are not, that they are not." From this founding principle the work developed a relativist and perhaps phenomenalist account of knowledge and reality, according to which things are as they appear to each individual perceiver, with no absolute, perceiver-independent truth. The very title *Truth* is thus pointed and provocative — a book on "truth" whose thesis is that truth is relative to the perceiver.

Our fullest knowledge of the work's argument comes from **Plato's dialogue *Theaetetus***, which quotes the famous opening and subjects the "measure" doctrine to extended, penetrating analysis and criticism — including the famous charge that the doctrine is *self-refuting* (if every belief is true for the one who holds it, then the belief that relativism is false is also true). Whatever the precise details of Protagoras's original arguments, *Truth* stands as the foundational document of philosophical relativism, and its influence — provoking the sustained response of Plato and shaping all subsequent debate about objectivity and knowledge — far outlasted the lost book itself.
works.On the Gods (fragments):
*On the Gods* (*Peri Theōn*) was the work of Protagoras that made him one of the most famous early **religious skeptics** (or agnostics) in the Western tradition — and, according to ancient tradition, brought about his prosecution for impiety and the public burning of his books in Athens. Like his other writings, it survives only in a celebrated **fragment** and later reports, but that fragment is one of the most remarkable statements of religious doubt to come down to us from antiquity.

The work reportedly opened with these famous and daring words: "**Concerning the gods, I am unable to know either that they exist or that they do not exist, or what they are like in form; for many things prevent knowledge — the obscurity of the subject and the shortness of human life.**" This is a statement of thoroughgoing **agnosticism**: Protagoras does not deny the gods (he is not an atheist), but declares that the question of their existence and nature is simply **beyond the reach of human knowledge**. The obstacles are twofold — the inherent obscurity and inaccessibility of such matters, and the brevity of human life, too short to resolve them.

This agnostic stance fit naturally with Protagoras's broader relativism and empiricism: if human beings are the measure of all things and knowledge is bound to human experience, then claims about transcendent, unobservable divine beings fall outside what we can know. The boldness of publicly professing such doubt in ancient Athens, where religion was woven into civic life, was considerable, and it reportedly cost Protagoras dearly. Though only a sentence survives, *On the Gods* stands as a landmark of ancient freethought and rational skepticism about religion — an early assertion that some of the deepest questions may simply exceed the limits of human knowledge.
